sheets.title: The Gauntlet
sheets.description: This is intentionally overbuilt. No list can guarantee an offer, because real interviews also test communication, speed, debugging, and judgment, but if you can solve this bank cold, explain tradeoffs cleanly, and implement without hints, you are very strongly prepared for FAANG-style DSA rounds. Sources used for calibration: LeetCode Top Interview 150, LeetCode Problemset, NeetCode Roadmap, CSES Problem Set, and cp-algorithms.

sheets.title: Extreme Mastery Map
sheets.description: This is the heavier replacement for a normal DSA list. It is built to cover the interview surface area, not just popular questions. No document can honestly guarantee a 100% pass rate, because interviews also test communication, speed, debugging, and judgment under pressure. But if you can solve this set cold, explain the invariants, and adapt to variants, you should be ready for almost any FAANG-style DSA interview. Primary calibration sources: LeetCode Problemset, LeetCode Top Interview 150, NeetCode Roadmap, CSES Problem Set, and cp-algorithms.

subgroups.title: In-Place Index Encoding And Cyclic Placement

items.text: Invariant: When values are bounded to [1, n], the array's own indices can serve as a hash table by negating or swapping into place.
Find All Duplicates in an Array
Invariant: A true majority element survives cancellation against every non-majority element when paired off.
Invariant: Rotating, zeroing, or traversing a matrix in place is an index-remapping exercise, not an allocation exercise.
Invariant: Store exactly the counts needed to answer "does the complement exist" in O(1) per element.
Check if Array Pairs Are Divisible by K
Find Players With Zero or One Losses
Invariant: Once you know each element's count, placement becomes a bucket/comparator problem rather than a search problem.
Group the People Given the Group Size They Belong To
Invariant: Two structures are equivalent iff their canonical (sorted-count or mapped) forms match exactly.
Determine if Two Strings Are Close
Invariant: Many "count elements satisfying X relative to position i" problems reduce to a running prefix/suffix aggregate.
Minimum Consecutive Cards to Pick Up

groups.title: Prefix Sums, Difference Arrays, Subarray Counting

items.text: Invariant: A subarray sum equals target iff two prefix sums differ by exactly target; store first/every occurrence in a hashmap.
Count All Subarrays With Given Sum
Number of Sub-arrays With Odd Sum
Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals k
Find Two Non-overlapping Sub-arrays Each With Target Sum
Invariant: A range +delta update is two point updates on a difference array, recovered by a single prefix-sum pass.
Invariant: A 2D block sum is four corner lookups into a precomputed 2D prefix table.
Invariant: Prefix reasoning generalizes to running products (with zero-resets), parity buckets, and circular wraparound.
Sum of All Odd Length Subarrays
Product of the Last K Numbers
Minimum Operations to Reduce X to Zero
Maximum Sum of Two Non-Overlapping Subarrays
Invariant: On a sorted array, advancing the pointer at the provably-wrong extreme is always safe and never skips the answer.
Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted
Minimize Maximum Pair Sum in Array
Invariant: A write pointer trailing a read pointer compacts or reorders an array in one pass without extra space.
Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II
Invariant: Comparing from both ends simultaneously verifies symmetry, or breaks it, in one pass.
Minimum Number of Moves to Make Palindrome
Invariant: Two pointers moving at different rates, or over different structures (list vs. BST-inorder), still preserve the sorted-search property.
Two Sum IV - Input is a BST
Invariant: A fixed window's aggregate updates in O(1) by adding the entering element and removing the one that leaves.
Maximum Number of Vowels in a Substring of Given Length
First Negative Integer in Every Window of Size K
Minimum Swaps to Group All 1's Together
Minimum Swaps to Group All 1's Together II
Maximum Points You Can Obtain from Cards

subgroups.title: Variable Window With A Budget Or Constraint

items.text: Invariant: Grow the window greedily; shrink from the left only when the constraint is violated, never re-scan from scratch.
Longest Subarray of 1's After Deleting One Element
Length of Longest Subarray With at Most K Frequency
Number of Substrings Containing All Three Characters
Count Complete Subarrays in an Array
Invariant: A deque that evicts dominated elements from the back keeps the window's max/min accessible at the front in amortized O(1).
Maximum Number of Robots Within Budget
Count Subarrays Where Max Element Appears at Least K Times
Invariant: Some windows need more than a running counter -- a bitmask, a value cap, or a companion data structure.
Frequency of the Most Frequent Element
Invariant: When the natural order isn't numeric or lexicographic, define a comparator that directly encodes the optimality criterion.
Invariant: Sorting collapses an assignment problem into a single left-to-right greedy pass.
Reduction Operations to Make the Array Elements Equal
Invariant: Sorting intervals by start (or end) turns overlap and containment questions into a single linear sweep.
Divide Intervals Into Minimum Number of Groups
Invariant: When intervals carry a value, a heap (or DP over sorted intervals) replaces plain greedy count-maximization.
Maximum Number of Events That Can Be Attended
Maximum Number of Events That Can Be Attended II
Invariant: Once sorted, the answer often lives in the gaps between consecutive elements, not the elements themselves.
Invariant: Sortedness lets you eliminate half the remaining indices on every comparison.

subgroups.title: Binary Search On Rotated/Perturbed Sorted Arrays

items.text: Invariant: At least one half of a rotated sorted array is always genuinely sorted; check which half to recurse into.
Search in Rotated Sorted Array II
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II

subgroups.title: Binary Search On A Property, Not An Exact Value

items.text: Invariant: Search a monotonic true/false predicate over index space rather than searching for one exact match.
Peak Index in a Mountain Array
Single Element in a Sorted Array
Invariant: If a candidate value satisfies the feasibility check, every value on one side of it also does -- monotonicity lets you binary search the answer itself.
Minimum Limit of Balls in a Bag
Find the Smallest Divisor Given a Threshold
Minimum Speed to Arrive on Time
Maximum Side Length of a Square with Sum Less than or Equal to Threshold
Sum of Mutated Array Closest to Target
Special Array With X Elements Greater Than or Equal X
Invariant: Row and column monotonicity let you eliminate a full row or column per comparison, not just one element.
Count Negative Numbers in a Sorted Matrix
Invariant: When the search space size is unknown up front, first find an exponential bound, then binary search inside it.
Invariant: A stack of open, unmatched tokens turns nested-validity checking into O(1) push/pop decisions.
Minimum Add to Make Parentheses Valid
Minimum Remove to Make Valid Parentheses
Invariant: A stack that pops on a matching adjacent element reduces a string/sequence by cancellation in one pass.
Remove All Adjacent Duplicates In String
Remove All Adjacent Duplicates in String II
Check If Word Is Valid After Substitutions
Invariant: A monotonic stack discards elements that can never be the answer for anything still to come, leaving O(n) total work.
Final Prices With a Special Discount in a Shop
Invariant: Instead of re-scanning per subarray, count how many subarrays each element controls as the extremum via its stack boundaries.
Invariant: Some problems are direct simulations of push/pop instructions or nested calls -- the stack literally is the state machine.
Build an Array With Stack Operations
Invariant: Recursive-descent or precedence-climbing parsing reduces to a stack of pending operators and operands.
Invariant: Augment a stack with extra bookkeeping (counts, ranges, secondary stacks) to answer more than just push/pop.
Design a Stack With Increment Operation
Invariant: A heap bounded to size k keeps only the k best candidates seen so far, discarding the rest immediately.
Reduce Array Size to the Half
Invariant: A heap holding one candidate per sorted source always exposes the next global minimum/maximum in log(k) time.
Invariant: Balancing a max-heap of the lower half against a min-heap of the upper half keeps the median at both roots.
Invariant: When the next best choice can only be judged by frequency or value, a max-heap makes the greedy choice cheap to repeat.
Total Cost to Hire K Workers
Invariant: A min-heap keyed by an expiry/finish time lets a greedy scheduler always evict or admit the most urgent item first.
Number of Orders in the Backlog
Invariant: Rather than remove a stale entry immediately, mark it and skip past it lazily the next time it surfaces at the top.
Invariant: A dummy node placed before the head removes special-casing whenever the head itself might be mutated or removed.
Remove Duplicates from Sorted List II
Reverse Nodes in Even Length Groups
Invariant: Relinking next-pointers around a boundary splices one structure into another without copying any values.
Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List
Invariant: A running accumulator (digit, bit, running sum) can be built during a single traversal instead of materializing an array first.
Convert Binary Number in a Linked List to Integer
Remove Zero Sum Consecutive Nodes from Linked List
Invariant: Two pointers separated by a fixed offset find the kth-from-end or swap symmetric nodes without a second pass.
Swapping Nodes in a Linked List

subgroups.title: Sorting And Ordered Insertion On Lists

items.text: Invariant: Classic sorting algorithms transfer to linked lists once array-index access is replaced by pointer traversal.
Invariant: To copy a graph-like structure (random pointers) in O(1) extra space, interleave the copy directly into the original.
Clone a Linked List with Next and Random Pointer
Next Greater Node In Linked List
Invariant: When you can't reach the head, a deletion or design task must be solved purely through local pointer surgery.
Delete Node in a Linked List
Invariant: Bucket or queue-based counters trimmed by a time/expiry boundary answer "how many in the last X" in amortized O(1).
Invariant: Maintaining a sorted structure, not just a hashmap, is what keeps rank/leaderboard/gap queries fast after every update.
Invariant: Modular arithmetic on read/write pointers turns a fixed array into a wraparound queue or deque.
Invariant: A back/forward navigation stack is really two stacks: one to go back, one to redo forward.
Invariant: Building the hashing or key-generation layer yourself exposes exactly what a library hashmap or shortener hides.
Invariant: An iterator interface can wrap lazy computation so elements are generated only as they're requested.
Invariant: Timestamped or expiring state needs a structure that tolerates updates arriving out of chronological order.
Invariant: Processing one full level at a time exposes width, per-level aggregates, and completeness properties that DFS would need extra bookkeeping to see.
Average of Levels in Binary Tree
Binary Tree Zigzag Level Order Traversal
Find Largest Value in Each Tree Row
Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node
Check Completeness of a Binary Tree
Invariant: Two trees match iff their shapes and values agree at every recursive step, optionally allowing a symmetric mirror or child swap.
Invariant: Carry a running value (sum, bitmask, string) down each root-to-leaf path and only finalize it once you reach a leaf.
Sum of Root To Leaf Binary Numbers
Pseudo-Palindromic Paths in a Binary Tree
Maximum Difference Between Node and Ancestor
Longest ZigZag Path in a Binary Tree
Invariant: An inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in sorted order, turning many BST problems into a sorted-sequence problem.
Insert into a Binary Search Tree
Invariant: A preorder (or postorder) value fixes the root; its position in the inorder sequence splits the rest into left/right subtrees.
Construct Binary Tree from Inorder and Postorder Traversal
Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Postorder Traversal
Invariant: Computing a value at a node from its already-computed children's results is a post-order accumulation, not a top-down pass.
Minimum Cost Tree From Leaf Values
Invariant: The number of valid BST shapes on n keys splits into an independent left-subtree-count times right-subtree-count sum over every root choice.
Invariant: Once you add parent pointers, a tree becomes an ordinary graph and BFS/DFS distance reasoning applies directly.
All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree
Second Minimum Node In a Binary Tree

Invariant: Binary tree serialization uses null markers for missing children, but an n-ary tree has no fixed child count -- it needs an explicit end-of-children marker, and threading a BST into a doubly linked list reuses the same left/right pointers as prev/next in place.
Convert BST to Sorted Doubly Linked List

Invariant: A BST's ordering lets you discard half the tree at every step when hunting for the single closest value, but hunting for the k closest values needs an in-order walk with a sliding window instead of repeated single-value searches.
Closest Binary Search Tree Value II

subgroups.title: Height-Based Leaf Peeling And Consecutive Runs

items.text: Invariant: Repeatedly stripping every current leaf layer is really a height computation read backwards, and tracking a consecutive value run down a root-to-leaf path only works if the recursion carries the run's current length as state, not just the running maximum.
Invariant: A trie shares common prefixes across all inserted words, so prefix-existence and prefix-count queries cost O(word length), not O(number of words).
Sum of Prefix Scores of Strings
Number of Distinct Substrings in a String
Invariant: Pruning a DFS the instant its current path leaves the trie avoids exploring any branch no dictionary word can complete.
Search a Word in a 2D Grid of Characters
Invariant: Allow bounded slack (one mismatch, a wildcard, both ends) by branching the trie search instead of scanning every word.
Invariant: Inserting words backward turns a suffix-containment question into an ordinary prefix (trie) question.
Invariant: A trie node can represent a filesystem directory or folder, not just a string prefix.
Invariant: Inserting numbers bit by bit into a binary trie lets you greedily maximize XOR by choosing the opposite bit at every level.
Invariant: Augmenting each trie node with a count or list turns it into an autocomplete engine or a unique-prefix generator.
Invariant: Constructing an output string token-by-token in a single pass avoids repeated concatenation costs.
Invariant: A valid character/token mapping must be consistent in both directions -- check both maps, not just one.
Invariant: Doubling a string turns rotation-containment into an ordinary substring search.
Invariant: A running balance counter decides the greedy split or edit point without ever needing to backtrack.
Split a String in Balanced Strings
Minimum Number of Swaps to Make the String Balanced
Invariant: A rolling hash or fixed window turns substring-existence or counting into O(1) work per shift.
Check If a String Contains All Binary Codes of Size K
Count Unique Characters of All Substrings of a Given String
Invariant: The prefix function encodes the longest border ending at every position, letting matching resume without ever re-scanning.
Invariant: Advancing a single pointer per source character checks subsequence-membership against many patterns simultaneously.
Invariant: Some string problems are really about text layout and parsing rules, not a single classic algorithm.
Invariant: Marking visited cells during DFS/BFS prevents recounting the same connected region.
Invariant: Seeding a BFS queue with every source at once computes each cell's distance to its nearest source in a single pass.
As Far from Land as Possible
Invariant: 2-coloring via BFS/DFS succeeds iff no edge ever connects two same-colored nodes.
Divide Nodes Into the Maximum Number of Groups
Invariant: The hardest step is often recognizing that indices, values, or states ARE the graph's nodes, before any BFS/DFS runs.
All Paths From Source to Target
Invariant: A huge but sparse grid needs an area-based cutoff or explicit adjacency, not a literal cell-by-cell visited scan.
Invariant: The same flood-fill/BFS machinery applies to character grids and abstract state graphs, not just 0/1 grids.
Invariant: A graph is a valid tree iff it has exactly n-1 edges AND is fully connected -- checking only one half of that lets a disconnected forest or an extra cycle slip through.

Invariant: A ball that keeps rolling until it hits a wall turns each BFS "move" into a whole line segment, not one cell -- and once moves have different lengths, plain BFS layers stop being distances and Dijkstra takes over.
Invariant: A node can be safely ordered only after every one of its prerequisites has already been ordered.
Detect Cycle in a Directed Graph
Invariant: Processing nodes in topological order guarantees every predecessor's DP value is already final before it's needed.
All Ancestors of a Node in a Directed Acyclic Graph
Invariant: Kahn's algorithm peels off zero-in-degree nodes layer by layer, which doubles as a dependency-resolution simulator.
Find All Possible Recipes from Given Supplies
Invariant: Collapsing each SCC into a single node turns any directed graph into a DAG.
Invariant: A 2-SAT instance reduces to building an implication graph and checking that no variable and its negation share an SCC.

Invariant: The graph's edges are not given -- they must be inferred one at a time from adjacent-word comparisons before any topological sort can run, and a single edge inference bug silently produces a wrong order.
Invariant: Union-find with path compression and union-by-rank answers "are these in the same component?" in near-O(1).
Find if Path Exists in Graph
Detect Cycle in an Undirected Graph
Invariant: Once elements are unioned into a component, apply one aggregate operation (sort, min, size check) to the whole group at once.
Minimum Score of a Path Between Two Cities
Count the Number of Complete Components

subgroups.title: DSU Union Trigger Beyond Direct Edges

items.text: Invariant: Two elements can be unioned via a shared property, such as a common factor or a distance threshold, instead of an explicit edge list.
Invariant: Process unions one at a time and answer a component-count or component-size query after each one.
The Earliest Moment When Everyone Become Friends
Invariant: When edges or nodes are being removed over time, process the queries backward and turn every removal into an addition.
Last Day Where You Can Still Cross
Invariant: Check a pre-union validity rule -- a restriction pair, a spread-impact estimate -- before or instead of performing the union.
Invariant: Kruskal's algorithm greedily adds the cheapest edge that doesn't create a cycle, verified by DSU on every step.
Reverse Delete Algorithm for Minimum Spanning Tree

Invariant: When land cells are added one at a time and you must report the live count of connected components after every addition, a fresh BFS per query is too slow -- DSU answers each query in near O(1) by unioning only the new cell with its already-land neighbors.
Invariant: Dijkstra greedily finalizes the closest unvisited node, which is provably correct when every edge weight is non-negative.
Invariant: Floyd-Warshall precomputes every pair's distance once so later point queries are O(1) lookups.
Invariant: Bellman-Ford relaxes every edge n-1 times; a further improvement on the nth pass means a negative cycle exists.
Invariant: When a plain (node) state isn't enough, expand the state to (node, extra resource) and run BFS/Dijkstra on that product graph.
Number of Restricted Paths From First to Last Node
Shortest Path in a Grid with Obstacles Elimination
Minimum Time to Visit a Cell In a Grid
Invariant: With uniform edge weights, plain BFS layer-by-layer expansion already computes shortest distance.
Nearest Exit from Entrance in Maze
Minimum Number of Visited Cells in a Grid
Invariant: A bottleneck shortest path optimizes the worst edge on the path, solved with a Dijkstra-like priority queue on that criterion.
Find the Safest Path in a Grid
Invariant: As shortcut edges are added one at a time, reuse prior distance work instead of rerunning shortest-path from scratch.
Shortest Distance After Road Addition Queries I
Shortest Distance After Road Addition Queries II
Invariant: An augmenting path alternates unmatched and matched edges to grow the current matching by exactly one.
Invariant: Repeatedly finding an augmenting path in the residual graph and saturating it computes the maximum flow.
Invariant: The max-flow value equals the min-cut capacity, and the flow decomposes into edge-disjoint augmenting paths.
Invariant: When matches carry different costs, the Hungarian algorithm finds the minimum-cost perfect assignment.
Invariant: Some problems are a flow/circulation problem in disguise even though they're solved with brute force at small scale.
Maximum Number of Achievable Transfer Requests
Invariant: Sorting by the right key makes the greedy choice at each step provably optimal -- no exchange of two elements can improve it.
Minimum Deletion Cost to Avoid Repeating Letters
Invariant: Some greedy choices are only obvious when you reason from the target backward to the start.
Invariant: Flipping the most impactful bit or sign first, greedily, dominates any other order.
Maximize Sum of Array After K Negations
Invariant: Adjusting the most/least frequent element first, greedily, resolves conflicts with the fewest total changes.
Minimum Deletions to Make Character Frequencies Unique
Minimum Increment to Make Array Unique
Divide Array in Sets of K Consecutive Numbers
Invariant: Trading the cheapest and most expensive remaining options against each other greedily is optimal at every step.
Invariant: Greedily extending the covered range as far as possible minimizes the total number of intervals used.
Invariant: Sorting by deadline or arrival time and greedily assigning the next resource produces a provably optimal schedule.
Invariant: Sorting by value-per-unit-cost and taking greedily from the top is optimal whenever items are divisible.
Invariant: A per-level seen-set dedupes branches without sorting, which matters when the original array order must be preserved.
Split a String Into the Max Number of Unique Substrings
Invariant: Assigning each element to one of several groups, with running-sum pruning, is a backtracking partition search.
Invariant: Building a number one digit at a time, branching over valid next digits, turns number-theory counting into a search tree.
Invariant: Marking a cell visited on the way in and explicitly un-marking it on the way back is what makes backtracking, backtrack.
Invariant: Tracking the best solution found so far lets you cut off any branch that can no longer beat it.
Tiling a Rectangle with the Fewest Squares
Invariant: Encoding a partial choice as a bitmask makes conflict-checking (overlap, reuse) an O(1) AND operation during the search.
Maximum Length of a Concatenated String with Unique Characters
Maximum Score Words Formed by Letters
Invariant: Distributing a limited resource among options, backtracking over how much goes where, prunes on remaining budget.
Invariant: Assign values one variable at a time, backtracking the instant any hard constraint is violated.

Invariant: A phone-lock pattern's move graph has "jump" constraints (2-to-8 is only legal if 5 is already used), so the backtracking check is on the edge, not just the destination cell -- and the board's symmetry lets you compute one corner and multiply.

subgroups.title: Greedy Approximation Vs Exact Minimum-Transaction Search

items.text: Invariant: A greedy match-the-largest-creditor-with-the-largest-debtor pass gives a valid, but not always minimal, transaction count -- the true minimum requires backtracking over which subsets of balances can be zeroed out together.

Invariant: Strobogrammatic strings are built from the center outward in valid digit pairs (0-0, 1-1, 6-9, 8-8, 9-6), so the recursion's base case depends on parity of the remaining length, not just reaching zero.
Invariant: The answer at position i depends only on a small fixed window of previous answers, so O(1) rolling space suffices.
Invariant: Every position offers a small fixed set of "step sizes"; sum or OR over the valid ones to get the recurrence.
Count Ways to Build Good Strings
Check if There is a Valid Partition for the Array
Invariant: Taking position i forces skipping a fixed window right after it, so dp[i] = value[i] + the best dp before that window.
Count Number of Ways to Place Houses
Invariant: At each element, decide independently whether to fold it into a running aggregate -- no skip-window bookkeeping needed.
Invariant: A handful of state variables (last value, count so far) is enough to count valid constructions position by position.
Number of Ways to Reach a Position After Exactly k Steps
Invariant: Trying every split point and combining the best of each side is a classic partition-DP shape.

subgroups.title: Sequential Cost DP With Adjacency Constraint

items.text: Invariant: Choosing option i at each step while forbidding the same choice as the previous step is a small-state running DP.

Invariant: With only 3 colors the DP state can just store the previous house's color, but once there are k colors, only tracking the running min and second-min cost (not all k values) keeps the transition O(1) instead of O(k) per house.
Invariant: Each item is taken or skipped exactly once; the DP state is (items considered so far, capacity remaining).
Invariant: Each item may be reused any number of times, so the recurrence stays on the same row/item when it's picked.
Invariant: Many problems that look unrelated (box stacking, cuboid stacking) are LIS wearing a different ordering relation.
Delete Columns to Make Sorted III
Invariant: Tracking an extra state bit (last direction, swapped-or-not) turns plain LIS into an alternating or paired-sequence DP.
Minimum Swaps to Make Sequences Increasing
Minimum Number of Removals to Make Mountain Array
Invariant: When the DP state depends on a derived key (a common difference) rather than a position, index the DP by that key.
Number of Sets of K Non-Overlapping Line Segments
Invariant: Splitting items into two groups to balance a target quantity is a subset-sum knapsack indexed by the difference.
Partition Array Into Two Arrays to Minimize Sum Difference
Invariant: When you choose a prefix count from each group rather than a single item, prefix sums combine with the knapsack DP.
Maximum Value of K Coins From Piles
Invariant: The same knapsack recurrence works in reverse to minimize cost/deficit instead of maximizing value.
Invariant: Aligning two strings character by character, keeping the best of match/skip-left/skip-right, generalizes to edit distance and beyond.
Minimum ASCII Delete Sum for Two Strings
Invariant: Track the number of mismatches allowed so far as an explicit DP dimension.
Count Substrings That Differ by One Character
Invariant: Precompute which substrings are palindromes, then DP over split points using that lookup table.
Maximum Product of the Length of Two Palindromic Subsequences
Invariant: DP over (position, run-length-so-far) captures how compression output size grows as characters are grouped.
Invariant: Counting distinct subsequences requires subtracting off subsequences double-counted via the last-seen-character trick.
Invariant: Count how many completions stay consistent with a given pattern (D/I sequence, digit bound) as you extend one position at a time.
Invariant: DP over prefix/suffix distinct-character counts turns a "good split" counting problem into two precomputed arrays.
Number of Good Ways to Split a String
Invariant: A running streak counter over a cyclic alphabet avoids re-deriving each substring's validity from scratch.
Invariant: Counting reachable permutations under an operation is a combinatorial DP over relative rank, not the values themselves.
Minimum Number of Operations to Make String Sorted
Invariant: Minimizing a "badness" cost across line breaks is an interval-style DP over split points in the word sequence.
Invariant: Each row's DP values depend only on the row directly above (or below), so the grid collapses to a rolling array.
Minimum Path Cost in a Grid
Maximum Non-Negative Product in a Matrix

subgroups.title: Grid DP: Path Existence/Counting Under Direction Constraints

items.text: Invariant: Directional constraints (one-way streets, must-visit-every-cell) turn plain path counting into a connectivity-aware DP.
Check if There is a Valid Path in a Grid
Number of Increasing Paths in a Grid
Invariant: Maximizing collected value while moving only in allowed directions is a grid DP with a value-not-cost objective.
Invariant: Trying every last split point inside a range and combining both sides is the matrix-chain-multiplication DP shape.
Invariant: With optimal play from both sides, dp[range] alternates whose turn it is and each side tries to maximize its own score.
Invariant: When more than one axis of state matters at once (position, speed, last color), the DP key becomes a tuple, not a single index.
Invariant: The DP state is which subset of items/cities/courses has been handled so far; transitions add one more to the mask.
Invariant: When the universe is small (<=20-30), directly enumerating every subset mask is fast enough to just check each one.
Invariant: DP over (position, tight-bound flag, extra constraint state) counts numbers with a property without enumerating them.
Count Integers With Even Digit Sum
Invariant: Sweeping cell by cell with a column-wide bitmask captures partially-placed tiles that span row boundaries.
Invariant: A Fenwick tree answers prefix-sum-style queries and point updates in O(log n) using only an implicit binary structure.
Count Good Triplets in an Array
Invariant: A segment tree stores a merge-able aggregate per node, so any range query is a small number of node visits.
Longest Substring of One Repeating Character
Invariant: Walking down the tree toward the first index satisfying a condition turns a scan into an O(log n) descent.
Invariant: For queries that never update the array, precomputing power-of-two-length ranges answers min/max queries in O(1).
Invariant: Compressing values to ranks lets a BIT answer "how many elements are less than X" style order-statistics queries.
Invariant: Nesting a BIT-of-BITs extends point-update/range-query to two dimensions at an extra log factor.
Invariant: Sorting queries by right endpoint and adding elements incrementally turns distinct-count-in-range into a BIT problem.
Invariant: A post-order pass accumulates each subtree's aggregate from its children before the parent needs it.
Invariant: Rerooting reuses the first DFS's results to answer the same question from every possible root in one extra pass.
Invariant: Hashing each subtree's canonical (sorted-child-hash) form lets you compare tree shapes in linear time.

subgroups.title: Binary Lifting For LCA On General Trees

items.text: Invariant: Precomputing 2^k-th ancestors lets any LCA or k-th-ancestor query resolve in O(log n).
Invariant: Sieving factorizations once up front turns many per-number number-theory queries into O(log) lookups.
Largest Component Size by Common Factor
Invariant: Fast exponentiation makes modular power/inverse computations O(log n) instead of O(n).
Invariant: A single bit-level observation (trailing bit parity, doubling) often collapses an apparent-simulation problem to O(log n).
Invariant: XOR is its own inverse, so prefix-XOR equality tests replace what would otherwise be an O(n^2) pairwise comparison.
Count Triplets That Can Form Two Arrays of Equal XOR
Minimum Number of Operations to Make Array XOR Equal to K
Find XOR Sum of All Pairs Bitwise AND
Minimum Flips to Make a OR b Equal to c
Invariant: Encoding a set (letters used, cells flipped) as an integer bitmask makes disjointness/overlap an O(1) AND check.
Minimum Number of Flips to Convert Binary Matrix to Zero Matrix
Invariant: Some encodings (UTF-8) are defined entirely by fixed bit-prefix rules, making validation a direct bit-pattern check.

subgroups.title: Randomization: Reservoir Sampling And Uniform Selection

items.text: Invariant: Reservoir sampling picks a uniformly random match from a stream without knowing its length in advance.
Invariant: The sign of a cross product classifies three points as a left turn, right turn, or collinear -- the basis of nearly all geometry algorithms.

subgroups.title: Computational Geometry: Area, Distance, Convex Hull

items.text: Invariant: The shoelace formula, squared-distance grouping, and monotone-chain hull construction all avoid costly sqrt/float operations.

sheets.title: Interview Failure Map
sheets.description: This is a correction to the previous DSA sheets. A giant random problem bank is not enough. The real target is the intersection of: 1. What public interview-prep lists repeatedly recommend. 2. What pattern taxonomies say you must recognize. 3. What actually causes candidates to fail under interview pressure. 4. What advanced topics cover the rare but brutal interviewer follow-ups. No prep document can honestly guarantee a 100% pass rate. But this map is built to make your prep much less random.

sections.title: Tier A: Trap Taxonomy -- Where Candidates Actually Break

items.text: Invariant: the trap is that an operation like multiply, add, or repeated doubling can silently overflow a fixed-width integer (or flip sign under negative operands / negative-mod semantics) while the algorithm's logic stays correct; survive it by reasoning in a wider type or an early-exit bound before the overflow point, and treating the sign/edge case (MIN_INT negation, negative operands) as a first-class branch, not an afterthought.
Divide Two Integers (the INT_MIN / -1 overflow edge case is the canonical version of this trap)

subgroups.title: Off-By-One / Boundary Traps In Binary Search

items.text: Invariant: the trap is that a binary search invariant proven correct for strictly-distinct, non-rotated data quietly breaks when duplicates or a rotation point are introduced, because `arr[mid] == arr[lo]` or `arr[mid] == arr[hi]` stops being decisive; survive it by naming, before you code, exactly which half the invariant still guarantees is sorted when a tie occurs, and shrinking the search space by one instead of guessing.
Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array
Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array II (duplicates break the "one half is always sorted" invariant)
Single Element in a Sorted Array (the boundary condition is an index-parity invariant, not a value comparison)

subgroups.title: Greedy That Looks Provably Right But Isn't

items.text: Invariant: the trap is trusting an intuitive greedy rule (always take the biggest/closest/cheapest option) without an exchange-argument proof, so it passes the examples you thought of and fails on a case where the "obviously correct" local choice forecloses a better global one; survive it by stating out loud why swapping any two adjacent choices can't improve the answer before you trust the greedy.
Candy (the two-pass greedy needs an exchange-argument proof most candidates never actually construct)

Invariant: the trap is recursion that looks like simple branching but actually revisits the same sub-state exponentially many times, so it "works" on the sample input and times out only once the input is large enough to expose the blow-up; survive it by identifying the full state that repeats (not just one parameter of it) and caching on that exact state before the first optimization pass, not after a TLE forces it.
Friends Pairing Problem (GeeksforGeeks -- naive recursion re-explores the same sub-count from two different branches; genuinely distinct from any LC problem already used elsewhere)
Word Break II (memoizing the boolean sub-question is not enough once you must also collect and return every valid sentence)

Invariant: the trap is that the loop order over items vs. capacity silently decides whether an item can be reused (unbounded knapsack) or used once (0/1 knapsack), and a 1-D in-place array can silently read a value this same iteration already overwrote; survive it by iterating the capacity dimension in decreasing order when items must not repeat and in increasing order when they may, and by naming, for every cell, whether you intend to read "last row's" or "this row's" value.
0/1 Knapsack (Naukri Code360 -- the base template isn't its own standalone LeetCode problem)
Unbounded Knapsack (Naukri Code360 -- same, no standalone LeetCode equivalent)
Coin Change II (combinations vs. permutations loop-order trap)
House Robber II (circular disguise of the same adjacency constraint)
Target Sum (assign +/- signs, disguised subset-sum reduction)
Partition to K Equal Sum Subsets

Invariant: the trap is that an algorithm designed for distinct elements silently produces repeated or missing results once duplicate values enter the input; survive it by sorting first and explicitly skipping over an equal value at the *same recursion level / same binary-search half*, not banning the value everywhere.
Search in Rotated Sorted Array II (duplicates break the "one half is always sorted" invariant that made the distinct-element version easy)

Invariant: the trap is mutating shared state (a path list, a visited grid, a used-digits set) on the way down and forgetting to undo that exact mutation on the way back up -- survive it by pairing every mutate-before-recursion with an identical undo-after-recursion in the same stack frame, so the container returns to the caller in the state it was handed.
Sudoku Solver (restoring both a cell value and per-row/col/box constraint state)

Invariant: the trap is sharing one mutable object (a node, a list, a reference) across recursive branches or forgetting to break aliasing when "copying," so the clone silently points back into the original structure and corrupts it on the next mutation; survive it by explicitly tracking a visited/cloned map from old node to new node before you follow a single additional pointer.
Clone Graph (recursing without a visited map infinite-loops on any cycle)
Populating Next Right Pointers in Each Node II (O(1)-space variant: the next-pointers you rely on to traverse the next level are exactly the ones you're still constructing on this level)

Invariant: the trap is treating "a cycle exists" and "this is the correct notion of cycle for this structure" as interchangeable facts; survive it by matching the detection rule to the actual semantics -- directed graphs need a recursion-stack/color check (not just a visited set), undirected graphs must ignore the immediate parent edge, and Floyd's meeting point is not the cycle's start until you reset one pointer to the head and walk both one step at a time.
Linked List Cycle II (meeting point is not the cycle start)
Graph Valid Tree (GeeksforGeeks/Naukri clone -- LC 261 is a real problem but premium-locked on leetcode.com)
Redundant Connection II (directed rooted tree: cycle detection entangled with a two-parent conflict)

Invariant: the trap is seeding the BFS queue with only one start node (or only "the" obvious source) when the grid actually has many simultaneous sources whose frontiers must expand together -- survive it by pushing every source cell into the queue at distance 0 *before* the first BFS step, so each queue "pop layer" represents one true global time-tick, not one node's personal distance.
Walls and Gates (Naukri Code360 -- LC 286 is a real problem but premium-locked on leetcode.com)
As Far from Land as Possible
Snakes and Ladders (single-source BFS where "layer = dice throw" is the trap)
Find Shortest Safe Route in a Matrix (GeeksforGeeks -- landmine danger-zone BFS, no direct LeetCode equivalent)
Shortest Bridge (DFS to find island one, then multi-source BFS from every cell of it)
Minimum Knight Moves (GeeksforGeeks "Steps by Knight" -- LC 1197 is premium-locked on leetcode.com)

subgroups.title: Union-Find Without Path Compression/Union-By-Rank -> Silent TLE

items.text: Invariant: the trap is a naive DSU (no path compression, no union by rank) that returns correct answers on small inputs and only reveals its near-linear-chain worst case once the graph is large enough to make every `find` walk a long parent chain; survive it by building path compression and union-by-rank into the very first version you write, not as a later optimization pass.
Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column (union happens on a shared row/column, not a direct edge -- easy to union the wrong pair of indices)
Bricks Falling When Hit (reverse-DSU: the naive forward simulation is the trap; the fix requires processing hits backward)

Invariant: the trap is applying an undirected-graph algorithm (plain two-coloring, plain Union-Find cycle check) to a directed graph, where a "back edge" to an already-visited node isn't automatically a cycle unless that node is still on the current recursion stack; survive it by tracking three node states (unvisited / in-progress / done) for directed graphs instead of the two states (visited / unvisited) that suffice for undirected ones.
Find Eventual Safe States (a node is only "unsafe" if it can reach a cycle -- which requires the three-state directed-graph coloring, not a plain visited set)

Invariant: the trap is a comparator that is correct on the primary key but produces a valid-looking, wrong answer once two elements tie, because the problem's real constraint (adjacency, distance, recency) only bites on the tie-break; survive it by writing the tie-break rule as an explicit second comparator key before you trust the heap's output.
Reorganize String (tie-breaking on frequency alone isn't enough to guarantee no two adjacent characters match)
Top K Frequent Words (frequency ties must break lexicographically, not arbitrarily)
Super Ugly Number (a heap without duplicate-suppression re-inserts the same value from multiple prime multipliers)

Invariant: the trap is silently treating "end of A equals start of B" as either always-merge or always-separate without checking which the problem actually wants -- survive it by writing the merge/overlap test as an explicit inequality (`<=` for touching-counts-as-overlap, `<` for touching-is-fine) and unit-testing the exact boundary case before trusting the sort-and-sweep.
Meeting Rooms II (Naukri Code360 "Minimum Number of Platforms" -- LC is premium-locked on leetcode.com)
My Calendar III (k-overlap sweep, boundary error compounds across many events)

Invariant: the trap is picking a shrink condition that is correct for "at most K" but then trying to reuse that same window logic directly for "exactly K" -- survive it by remembering exactly(K) = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1), and by re-deriving the shrink-while condition from scratch for every new constraint instead of copy-pasting the last problem's window.
Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters (Naukri Code360 -- LC 340 is premium-locked on leetcode.com)
Longest K Unique Characters Substring (GeeksforGeeks -- "exactly K", not "at most K"; must return -1 if impossible, no direct LeetCode equivalent)
Subarrays with K Different Integers (the canonical atMost(K)-atMost(K-1) boss problem)

Invariant: the trap is conflating "the best value achievable through this node as a bridge between two of its branches" with "the single best value this node is allowed to hand up to its parent"; survive it by having the recursive call return only the one-sided, parent-usable value while a separate outer/global variable is updated with the two-sided (through-node) combination at every node.
Longest Path With Different Adjacent Characters

Invariant: the trap is evaluating strictly left-to-right and only discovering precedence rules exist once `*`/`/` binds tighter than `+`/`-`, or once nested parentheses require a stack frame per depth level rather than one global state; survive it by deciding up front whether you need a two-stack (operator/operand) parser or a recursive-descent parser, and never trying to patch precedence into a single flat left-to-right scan.
Basic Calculator II (no parentheses, but now precedence between `*`/`/` and `+`/`-` is the entire problem)
Different Ways to Add Parentheses (every possible precedence grouping simultaneously, via divide-and-conquer)

subgroups.title: Wrong-Complexity Brute Force That Passes Small Tests But Fails At Scale

items.text: Invariant: the trap is an O(n^2) approach that looks correct and passes every example you hand-check, so the failure only shows up against the judge's large hidden tests; survive it by stating the target complexity from the problem's constraints *before* writing code, not after a TLE.
Two Sum (nested-loop brute force vs. one-pass hash map)
Container With Most Water (checking every pair vs. the two-pointer argument for why the shorter side can always be discarded)
Trapping Rain Water (per-index brute force recomputes both directions' max on every step)
Longest Consecutive Sequence (sort-and-scan looks like the "obvious" O(n log n) fix, but the real target is O(n) via a hash set and only expanding from sequence starts)

Invariant: the trap is that a prefix sum, running total, or intermediate result can go negative, and languages differ on what `%` returns for negative operands, so a remainder-equality check silently misses valid answers; survive it by normalizing every modulo result into `[0, k)` with `((x % k) + k) % k` before comparing or using it as a hash-map key.
Subarray Sums Divisible by K (negative prefix sums must still map to the same remainder bucket as their positive counterparts)
K-Concatenation Maximum Sum (combines Kadane's with a mod-10^9+7 output on a value that can be enormous before the mod is applied)

Invariant: the trap is that right-shifting a negative number, or treating a language's fixed-width integer as if it had infinite bits, silently changes which bit ends up where; survive it by masking to the exact bit width the problem specifies (`& 0xFFFFFFFF`) before any shift, and reasoning about the sign bit as data, not noise.
Sum of Two Integers (bitwise add without `+`; carry propagation on negative operands is the whole difficulty)
Single Number II (bit-counting mod 3 across 32 bit positions; sign bit must be handled the same as any other bit)

sheets.title: System Design Mastery
sheets.description: This is a practice document for system design, low-level design, and the non-DSA technical material that large tech companies commonly test. No honest prep plan can guarantee a 100% pass rate, but this is designed to make passing highly likely if you can execute the drills under interview conditions. Core references for calibration: System Design Primer Google SRE Book Google SRE Workbook AWS Well-Architected Framework Amazon Builders' Library Designing Data-Intensive Applications Martin Fowler Architecture Guide PostgreSQL Documentation Kafka Documentation Redis Documentation MDN HTTP Overview

"After receiving a request, a web server first checks if the cache has the available response... This caching strategy is called a read-through cache... Least-recently-used (LRU) is the most popular cache eviction policy." — Alex Xu, *System Design Interview* (Vol. 1), Ch. 1

"A CDN is a network of geographically dispersed servers used to deliver static content... The origin returns image.png to the CDN server, which includes optional HTTP header Time-to-Live (TTL)." — Alex Xu, *System Design Interview* (Vol. 1), Ch. 1

"Weak Consistency: After a write, reads may or may not see it... Eventual Consistency: After a write, reads will eventually see it (typically within milliseconds)... Strong Consistency: After a write, reads will see it. Data is replicated synchronously." — System Design Primer

sheets.title: SQL Gauntlet
sheets.description: This is the SQL counterpart to the DSA Gauntlet: real, verified, free practice problems organized by concept into Foundation/Reinforcement/Boss ladders. Sources: LeetCode's Database problem set, HackerRank's SQL domain, StrataScratch's free problem set, and DataLemur's free problem set -- every problem link was checked to make sure it's real, free, and not premium-locked. Neither StrataScratch nor DataLemur tags problems by SQL technique the way this sheet does -- every problem from those two sources was categorized by hand based on what it actually tests, not by a site-provided tag. The goal is full-platform coverage of the free tier: working through this sheet should leave nothing free on either platform looking unfamiliar. For the premium-locked problems too (StrataScratch's are still solvable without a subscription, just not auto-graded), see the SQL Extreme Mastery sheet. Pure practice ladders only for now -- the theory side (indexing, normalization, transactions, query optimization, and reading references) will come back as its own separate sheet once there's real source material to build it from properly. **Organized by pattern, not syntax.** Interviewers don't think "this is a ROW_NUMBER question" -- they think "this is a latest-record-per-user problem." Sections below are grouped by the shape of the question you'll actually be asked, matching the SQL Extreme Mastery sheet's organization. Every problem still carries a `{tag}` naming the specific syntax/technique that solves it, so you can jump straight to "everything that uses LAG" if that's what you're drilling. The one exception is the opening Fundamentals section -- those are the atoms (SELECT, WHERE, string/date functions) that every pattern below is built out of, so syntax *is* the pattern at that level.

items.text: Invariant: before touching a single join, aggregate, or subquery, you need SELECT/WHERE/ORDER BY to be pure muscle memory on one table -- everything else in this sheet is built on top of this.
Weather Observation Station 3 (HackerRank -- WHERE with a modulo condition)
Higher Than 75 Marks (HackerRank -- WHERE + ORDER BY combined)
Name of Employees (HackerRank -- string + ORDER BY)

Invariant: AND/OR predicates in WHERE are evaluated row-by-row before any grouping or sorting happens, so getting the right rows back is a matter of correct boolean composition, not which columns you SELECT.
Find all athletes who were older than 40 years when they won either Bronze or Silver medals (StrataScratch)

Invariant: String functions like SUBSTR/CONCAT/LIKE/REGEXP operate on the literal contents of a column, so a pattern that "looks right" (e.g. `'DIAB1%'`) can silently miss valid matches unless you account for every position the target substring is allowed to appear in.

Invariant: Date/time arithmetic (DATEDIFF, timestamp subtraction) only compares two values you've already lined up in the same row, so "yesterday vs. today" or "time to complete a process" problems usually need a self-join or window function before any subtraction can happen.
User Activity for the Past 30 Days I
Average Time of Process per Machine
Average Post Hiatus Part 1 (DataLemur -- LAG for gap between a user's posts)

Invariant: Truncating a date to a coarser grain (DATE_FORMAT to year-month, pulling out the month) is what lets you GROUP BY or pivot on a period instead of an exact timestamp -- get the truncation expression wrong and every row ends up in its own bucket.
Average Review Ratings (DataLemur -- monthly bucketing + AVG)

subgroups.title: COALESCE/IFNULL for Missing Values & Safe Division

items.text: Invariant: A LEFT JOIN manufactures NULLs for every unmatched row, so any arithmetic, ratio, or display built on the joined column must explicitly convert NULL with COALESCE/IFNULL or the NULL silently propagates and poisons the result.
Replace Employee ID With The Unique Identifier

Invariant: CASE WHEN evaluates its branches in order and returns NULL for any row that matches no WHEN and has no ELSE, so a bucketing/categorization query has to be checked for exhaustiveness, not just the correctness of each individual branch.
Calculate Samantha's and Lisa's total sales revenue (StrataScratch)

subgroups.title: Three-Valued Logic Gotchas (NULL in WHERE/JOIN/NOT IN)

items.text: Invariant: NULL is neither TRUE nor FALSE -- `column <> NULL` and `NOT IN (subquery containing a NULL)` both quietly evaluate to UNKNOWN/empty instead of erroring, so the safe fix is explicit `IS NULL` checks or `NOT EXISTS` rather than negated equality/IN.
Employees Earning More Than Their Managers
Employees Whose Manager Left the Company

Invariant: Aggregate functions collapse many rows into one per group, so the choice of MIN/MAX/AVG/SUM determines which underlying row (or blend of rows) the answer actually represents -- the wrong aggregate gives a plausible-looking but incorrect number.
Percentage of Users Attended a Contest
The Blunder (HackerRank -- aggregate comparison against a formula-corrupted column)

Invariant: Grouping by a combination of columns treats each unique tuple as its own bucket, so a `HAVING COUNT(*) >= k` threshold is really asking "how many rows share this whole combination of values," not just one column in isolation.
Actors and Directors Who Cooperated At Least Three Times
Group Sold Products By The Date
Find the number of inspections for each risk category by inspection type (StrataScratch)
Friend Requests II: Who Has the Most Friends

Invariant: WHERE filters raw rows before aggregation runs, while HAVING filters the already-aggregated groups afterward, so any condition that references an aggregate (COUNT, SUM) has to live in HAVING -- putting it in WHERE either errors or silently filters on the wrong thing.
Managers with at Least 5 Direct Reports
List the Products Ordered in a Period

Invariant: `SUM(CASE WHEN category = 'x' THEN value ELSE 0 END)` turns one row-per-category table into one column-per-category row without a native PIVOT operator -- each CASE branch is a hand-built column. This is one of the single most-used techniques in real interviews: any time the output needs "one row per entity, one column per category," conditional aggregation is almost always the answer.

subgroups.title: SUM(CASE WHEN...) / COUNT(CASE WHEN...) as Hand-Built Columns

items.text: Occupations (HackerRank -- same pivot concept, different engine/dataset)
Odd and Even Measurements (DataLemur -- pivot with a per-day aggregation twist)

Invariant: INNER JOIN keeps only rows with a matching key on both sides, so any row whose foreign key doesn't exist in the other table silently disappears from the result -- exactly what you want for "complete records only," and exactly the bug when you don't notice the drop.
The Number of Employees Which Report to Each Employee
Find all Lyft rides which happened on rainy days before noon (StrataScratch)

subgroups.title: LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER JOIN & Finding Unmatched Rows

items.text: Invariant: A LEFT/RIGHT/FULL OUTER JOIN preserves the non-matching side's rows and fills the other side with NULL, so "find rows with no match" and "count including zeros" both reduce to filtering or aggregating over those NULL-filled rows instead of trying to enumerate absence directly.
Customer Who Visited but Did Not Make Any Transactions

Invariant: `NOT IN` against a subquery that can produce even one NULL silently returns an empty result for the whole query (three-valued logic strikes again), while `NOT EXISTS` is immune because it only ever tests row existence, never compares against the NULL value itself.
Find all posts which were reacted to with a heart (StrataScratch)
Find Customers With Positive Revenue this Year

subgroups.title: Anti-Join Patterns (Finding Rows With No Match)

items.text: Invariant: "find rows in A with no match in B" is the same shape whether you write it as `NOT EXISTS`, `LEFT JOIN ... WHERE B.key IS NULL`, or `NOT IN` -- pick based on NULL-safety and the query planner, not because one is "more correct."
Find Books with No Available Copies
items.subtag: LEFT JOIN + IS NULL / NOT EXISTS

items.text: Placements (HackerRank -- anti-join style filtering across 3 joined tables)

Invariant: "find entities that have every item in a set" (all skills, all products) can't be done with a single equality check -- it's a `HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT matched_item) = (total items required)` pattern, or an aggregate-comparison subquery, not a simple WHERE filter.
Data Science Skills (DataLemur -- candidates who have every required skill)
Supercloud Customer (DataLemur -- customer who purchased every product)

subgroups.title: Comparing a Row to a Related Row in the Same Table

items.text: Invariant: joining a table to itself under two different aliases lets you compare each row against a related row in the same table (a manager, the previous day, a duplicate email) as if they were separate tables.
Well Paid Employees (DataLemur -- self-join to a manager's salary)
Cards Issued Difference (DataLemur -- self-join comparing two card versions)
Binary Tree Nodes (HackerRank -- self-referencing parent/child table)
Repeated Payments (DataLemur -- self-join within a date window)

Invariant: chaining several JOINs pulls related facts scattered across normalized tables back into one row, but each additional join can silently multiply row counts if the join keys aren't as unique as assumed.
The Report (HackerRank -- multi-table join with grade-based CASE bucketing)
Invariant: a subquery in SELECT or WHERE runs first and collapses to a single value (or a set IN can test against), so the outer query never needs to know how that value was computed.
Weather Observation Station 13 (HackerRank -- scalar subquery inside a range check)
The PADS (HackerRank -- subquery-built label per row)
Salary Less Than Twice The Average (StrataScratch -- WHERE against an aggregate subquery)
Harry Potter and Wands (HackerRank -- subquery + multi-condition filtering)

Invariant: a correlated subquery re-runs once per outer row because it references a column from the outer query, so it's really a per-row lookup in disguise -- correct but potentially slow if the inner query itself isn't cheap.
Top Competitors (HackerRank -- correlated count per contestant)
Product Price at a Given Date
Challenges (HackerRank -- correlated subquery with a tie-breaking rule)

groups.title: Naive Top-N via ORDER BY / LIMIT

items.text: Invariant: LIMIT/OFFSET simply chop the already-sorted result set, so safely returning "the Nth highest value" requires collapsing duplicates (DISTINCT) or ranking with a window/subquery -- a naive `LIMIT N-1,1` silently gives the wrong row whenever ties exist.
Customer Placing the Largest Number of Orders

items.subtag: Correlated Subquery -- Top-N per group without a window fn

items.text: Invariant: DISTINCT and COUNT(DISTINCT ...) collapse rows down to unique values before you count or list them, so "find the duplicates" or "find the unmatched items" problems hinge on precisely what set membership means for the data, not on filtering individual rows.
Unique Users Per Client Per Month (StrataScratch)
Number of Unique Subjects Taught by Each Teacher

Invariant: ROW_NUMBER always breaks ties arbitrarily into distinct integers, RANK leaves gaps after a tie (1,1,3), and DENSE_RANK doesn't (1,1,2) -- picking the wrong one silently changes which row "wins" a tie or how many distinct tiers exist. This is probably the single most common SQL interview pattern: "highest salary per department," "latest order per customer," "top 3 products per category" are all the same shape -- PARTITION BY the group, ORDER BY the tiebreaker, filter to rank <= N.
Second Highest Salary (StrataScratch -- solve with DENSE_RANK instead of LIMIT/OFFSET)
User's Third Transaction (DataLemur -- ROW_NUMBER = 3 per user)
The Most Recent Orders for Each Product
Highest Salary In Department (StrataScratch -- top-N per group via window ranking)

Invariant: "the most recent X per Y" and "the first X per Y" are the same shape as greatest-N-per-group with N=1, but they show up so often as their own phrasing ("latest login," "first purchase," "most recent activity") that it's worth drilling as its own reflex: MAX/MIN + GROUP BY when you only need the value, ROW_NUMBER/FIRST_VALUE + PARTITION BY when you need the whole row.
Rising Temperature {Self-Join on DATE_ADD -- day-over-day, see Time-Series section}

subgroups.title: Comparing a Row to Its Neighbor, and Spotting When a Value Changes

items.text: Invariant: LAG/LEAD fetch a value from a different row within the same ordered partition without a self-join -- the offset (default 1) is how many rows away, and a missing neighbor (start/end of partition) returns NULL unless a default is given. The same function underlies two distinct interview phrasings: "compare this row to the previous one" (period-over-period change, time between events) and "did this value change from the last time we saw it" (status transitions, salary changes) -- both are `LAG(col) OVER (PARTITION BY entity ORDER BY time) <> col`.
Monthly Percentage Difference (StrataScratch -- LAG for month-over-month change)

items.subtag: UNION + GROUP BY/ORDER BY (no LAG needed, but same ladder)

subgroups.title: Finding Where a Sequence of Consecutive Values Breaks

items.text: Invariant: subtracting a row's own rank/row-number from its ordered value collapses every run of consecutive values to the same constant -- that constant becomes a free grouping key for "find each island of consecutive rows."
SQL Project Planning (HackerRank -- the canonical gaps-and-islands problem)
User Shopping Sprees (DataLemur -- consecutive-day purchase streaks)
15 Days of Learning SQL (HackerRank -- consecutive-day submission streak)

groups.title: Median and Mode Without a Native Function

subgroups.title: Finding the Middle or Most-Frequent Value By Hand

items.text: Invariant: without a native `MEDIAN()`, the median is "sort the values, then average the one or two values sitting at the middle rank" -- built from ROW_NUMBER/PERCENTILE_CONT plus a COUNT to locate the middle, not a single built-in call in most engines that matter for interviews.
Compressed Mean (DataLemur -- weighted average from a bucketed representation)
Compressed Mode (DataLemur -- statistical mode via GROUP BY + ORDER BY + LIMIT)
items.subtag: GROUP BY + ORDER BY COUNT DESC

items.text: Invariant: a CTE is just a named, reusable subquery scoped to one statement -- it doesn't change what's computable, only how readable the multi-step logic is, and it can be referenced more than once without repeating the subquery text.
Spotify Streaming History (DataLemur -- multi-step CTE chain)
Senior Managers (DataLemur -- CTE + self-join over a reporting hierarchy)

subgroups.title: UNION vs UNION ALL & Combining Differently-Shaped Result Sets

items.text: Invariant: UNION implicitly deduplicates its combined result (an expensive sort/hash step) while UNION ALL keeps every row including duplicates; and when the two source tables don't naturally share a shape, you build that shared shape explicitly with literal/placeholder columns before unioning.
User Activity for the Past 30 Days II
Duplicate HR Department Employees (StrataScratch -- UNION ALL to intentionally duplicate rows)
Make the friends network symmetric (StrataScratch -- UNION to mirror a relationship both directions)
Find all possible varieties which occur in either of the winemag datasets (StrataScratch)

subgroups.title: Did the Same User Come Back?

items.text: Invariant: "retention" and "reactivation" questions are a self-join (or LAG) of a user's activity in period N against their activity in period N-1 or their very first period -- there's no single-row answer, the comparison IS the query.
Second Day Confirmation (DataLemur -- did the user act again exactly one day later)
Reactivated Users (DataLemur -- came back after a long gap)

subgroups.title: Turning Two Counts Into a Rate

items.text: Invariant: every "percentage" or "rate" question is two aggregates (a numerator COUNT/SUM and a denominator COUNT/SUM) divided against each other, almost always with an explicit numeric CAST to avoid integer division silently truncating to 0.
Find the percentage of shipable orders (StrataScratch)
Card Launch Success (DataLemur -- rate calculation gated by a time window)
Trips and Users (the classic multi-join banned-users ratio problem)

sheets.title: SQL Extreme Mastery
sheets.description: This is the exhaustive companion to the SQL Gauntlet: every remaining problem across LeetCode's Database category, HackerRank's SQL domain, DataLemur, and StrataScratch's full Analytical question bank -- including premium/locked ones, not just the free tier the Gauntlet sticks to. On premium content specifically: StrataScratch lets you write and run a query against every problem's real dataset for free, you just don't get its own pass/fail judge without a subscription -- since it still returns real output, you can eyeball it against the problem statement yourself (or paste your query + output into an LLM to sanity-check correctness) instead of skipping the problem entirely. LeetCode Premium problems are included the same way: real problems, worth attempting even without unlocking the official checker. No Foundation/Reinforcement/Boss ladders here -- this sheet is organized into broad topic buckets by an automated pass over each problem's title (StrataScratch and DataLemur don't expose a technique tag, and most of these titles can't be read in full without paying, so this is best-effort classification, not a hand-verified read-through like the Gauntlet). Difficulty, source platform, and company (where known) are tagged on each item.

sheets.title: CSES Complete Map
sheets.description: This workbook maps every current CSES Problem Set task to a technique family, prerequisite runway, beginner/intermediate/pro solidifiers, and mutation drills. It is meant to help you solve the whole CSES set without treating it as 400 unrelated puzzles. Source catalog: CSES Problem Set. The catalog was scraped during this session from the live task list. Total CSES tasks mapped: 400.

sheets.title: System Design Content Zone
sheets.description: A curriculum-first system design index -- structured the way a proper course would be (fundamentals, then building blocks, then patterns, then case studies grouped by domain), not just a dump of whatever links happened to be free. Every linked title points to a verified free page (compiled by hand from ByteByteGo, Design Gurus, Educative's Grokking course, Hello Interview, AlgoMaster, the System Design Primer, and roadmap.sh). Topics without a link yet are marked `(yet to be linked)` -- there are a lot of them by design; getting the curriculum shape right came first, links get filled in as they're found. Style matches the Extreme Mastery sheets: broad topic buckets, no Foundation/Reinforcement/Boss ladders, `{Platform}` tag per item.
